utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality output overall. Both runs pass all five verifier checks and satisfy J1.1, J1.2, J1.3, and J1.6 comparably. However, wi_skills clearly outperforms wo_skills on the defense-domain-specific criteria: J1.4 (RCM failure mode classification with MIL-STD-3034 logic) and J1.5 (CBM+ maturity assessment against the DoD framework). The wo_skills report is data-rich and statistically thorough — it leverages a Python script to process all 2,613 work orders and produces quantitative tables — but it remains a general statistical summary rather than a true defense-readiness assessment. It lacks RCM failure distribution classification, does not assess CBM+ maturity beyond a glossary entry, and does not structure recommendations within the four-horizon roadmap tied to regulatory frameworks (MIL-STD-3034, DoD CBM+ Guidebook). The wi_skills report, driven by the defense-maintenance skill, correctly applies DoD-specific frameworks and delivers ORE-ready analysis that the brigade commander can act on at the command level. Conclusion: wi_skills is meaningfully more effective for this task.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is substantially more effective than wo_skills. Both runs correctly compute basic readiness metrics (MC rate, NMCS/NMCM split, top WUC contributors) and pass J1.1 and J1.2. However, wi_skills uniquely delivers: (1) OPTEMPO correlation linking surge periods to accelerated MC decline (J1.3); (2) RCM failure distribution classification with matched maintenance strategy recommendations per MIL-STD-3034 (J1.4); (3) explicit CBM+ maturity assessment with HUMS integration gap analysis per the DoD CBM+ framework (J1.5); and (4) recommendations tied to specific quantified data points such as the 15% manning shortfall, 74% CLS compliance, and 6.2-hour QC backlog (J1.6). The wo_skills report is competent at data summarization and quantitative readiness proxies but lacks the regulatory framework grounding, failure mode classification, and structured multi-horizon roadmap that define an ORE-ready defense maintenance assessment. wi_skills scored 6/6 versus wo_skills at 2/6 on the judge evaluation items.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors observed in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents encountered the absence of pandas and correctly fell back to stdlib csv parsing. The wo_skills run correctly noted the defense-maintenance skill was not available and proceeded without it. The wi_skills run read and applied the SKILL.md guidance before analysis.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality PMR assessments that satisfied all six judge evaluation criteria. Both reports computed the same core metrics (117.1-day actual vs 94.1-day target TAT, 38.9% penalty rate, 80% fill rate, 54.5% 91A journeyman fill, 75.2% safety TCTO compliance, 12 components above 0.65 CBR). However, wi_skills demonstrates higher analytical depth and PMR-readiness in several dimensions: (1) it explicitly invokes the SKILL.md framework and applies it structurally, (2) it introduces an 'effective fill' metric (65.8% after certification attrition) not present in wo_skills, (3) it frames the 38.9% penalty rate explicitly against the AMCOM 33% contract-review threshold by name, (4) it uses a more precise 13-quarter deficiency streak characterization, (5) it provides more granular action timelines (30/90/180-day) and more explicit AR 750-1 policy citations throughout. The wo_skills report is also competent and data-accurate but reads slightly more as a data summary with conclusions, whereas wi_skills reads more as a policy-grounded executive assessment with regulatory consequence framing. Overall: wi_skills produces a marginally higher-quality output better suited for executive PMR consumption, consistent with the expected skill differentiation.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully and produced valid output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced comprehensive, data-driven CBM+ assessments that passed all six verifier checks and satisfied all six judge evaluation criteria. Both reports applied structured DoD CBM+ maturity frameworks, identified maintenance-induced failure patterns (MRGB early failures), performed PM interval correlation analysis with extension/conversion recommendations, reframed cannibalization as a supply stockage signal, flagged stale technical documents by type and block, and delivered phased 36-month roadmaps. The wi_skills report (18,964 bytes) invoked the /defense-maintenance skill and structured its analysis around explicit charter gate criteria with precise proxy metrics. The wo_skills report (24,780 bytes) was more voluminous, included additional detail on cannibalization component breakdown (listing top components by count: TRGB 130, MRGB 126, etc.) and explicitly labeled 70 unique modification sets. Both demonstrate similar analytical depth on the evaluated dimensions. The qualitative difference is subtle: wi_skills more tightly structured its findings around the charter's phase gate language, while wo_skills provided more granular component-level cannibalization data. Overall, the two runs are roughly equivalent in quality for the evaluated criteria, with wi_skills showing slightly better alignment to the DoD CBM+ maturity framework language and wo_skills providing marginally more detailed component-level supply chain specifics. wi_skills is marginally more effective due to its direct skill invocation and tighter phase-gate framing.

utility_details.judge_error: No agent timeout or execution errors detected in either run. Both runs completed successfully and produced output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive PMR-ready assessments that passed all six judge evaluation criteria. Both applied LORA CBR thresholds, distinguished safety vs operational TCTOs, identified the 91A journeyman gap, quantified PBL penalty rates, and synthesized findings into integrated risk assessments. The primary differentiator is that wi_skills explicitly invoked the defense-maintenance skill (Step 1 shows skill retrieval) and produced a structured, heavily regulated analytical framework with explicit AR 750-1 paragraph citations embedded throughout, a formal CBR calculation methodology labeled as LORA, and a validation pass checking 32 content requirements. wo_skills achieved comparable substantive depth through direct Python computation and produced a slightly larger file (45KB vs 39KB), with equivalent quantitative findings. The quality difference is marginal — both are PMR-ready — but wi_skills demonstrates slightly superior regulatory framing and analytical rigor due to the skill's domain-specific framework. Overall: wi_skills is marginally more effective due to richer regulatory context integration, though both outputs would satisfy AMC leadership requirements.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective than wo_skills. Both runs produced comprehensive reports passing all 6 verifier checks, but there are qualitative differences in analytical depth. The wi_skills run explicitly applied RCM logic to identify maintenance-induced failure patterns (J3.2) from the hours-since-last-PM temporal correlation data and systematically differentiated PM interval extension vs CBM conversion candidates (J3.3) — the two most specialized analytical dimensions. The wo_skills run, while producing a larger file (52KB vs 44KB) with good breadth of coverage on sensor inventory, configuration management, cannibalization quantification, and roadmap structure, did not demonstrate the same depth of maintenance-induced failure pattern detection or RCM-based task differentiation. The skill's CBM+ and RCM methodological guidance clearly enabled the wi_skills agent to perform the higher-order analytical tasks that distinguish a strategic modernization assessment from a data inventory report.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced comprehensive PMR-ready assessments that passed all verifier checks and met all six judge evaluation criteria. The wi_skills run (22,646 bytes) is more detailed than the wo_skills run (15,966 bytes), but critically, the wo_skills agent independently computed the same key metrics (CBR thresholds, TAT figures, TCTO compliance percentages, PBL penalty rates, workforce fill rates) and applied the same policy frameworks without invoking the skill. The wi_skills output is marginally more structured with a broader executive summary and more explicit policy citations, but both outputs are usable for executive-level program decisions. Overall, wi_skills is slightly higher quality in depth and organization, but the qualitative difference is modest given that both outputs satisfy all evaluation criteria.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective overall. Both runs produced comprehensive assessments passing all 6 verifier checks, and both scored well on CBM+ maturity mapping (J3.1), cannibalization supply framing (J3.4), technical data currency analysis (J3.5), and phased roadmap construction (J3.6). However, wi_skills uniquely scored on J3.2 (maintenance-induced failure detection) by analyzing the temporal relationship between unscheduled failures and preceding PM events using hours_since_last_pm data — identifying specific components like MRGB where failures cluster shortly after PM events, which is the most analytically sophisticated and differentiating finding. wi_skills also scored on J3.3 (PM interval optimization using failure correlation) by differentiating interval-extension candidates from CBM-conversion candidates with data rationale, while wo_skills only enumerated stale PM tasks without performing correlation analysis. The skill-guided run demonstrates the RCM methodology and maintenance-induced failure pattern recognition called for in the expected outcomes, resulting in a final score of 6/6 vs. 4/6 for wo_skills.
